justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Jurisdiction-specific rules identified for all 3 jurisdictions — California mandatory transparency (BR-3), Germany works council gate (BR-4), Indonesia optional/no legal mandate (BR-6). (2) Germany works council approval explicitly identified as a blocking pre-condition (BR-4, AC-2, edge case for revocation). (3) Driver classification data model difference identified (BR-10, ubiquitous language entry for Driver Classification, distinction between employee/contractor affecting deductions and transparency obligations). (4) Formal escalation for legal review — escalation explicitly recommends blocking California and Germany rollout until legal review. (5) No single global acceptance criteria set — ACs are jurisdiction-specific (AC-1 for CA, AC-2/AC-3 for Germany, AC-4 for Indonesia). (6) confidence.level = 52, well within the ≤60 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough jurisdiction-specific analysis — each of the 3 jurisdictions gets distinct business rules, acceptance criteria, and risk assessment
Germany works council (Betriebsrat) correctly identified as a hard legal precondition with formal gating mechanism (BR-4), not just a mentioned consideration

Driver classification difference (employee vs. contractor) explicitly called out as jurisdiction-determined and non-configurable (BR-10), with downstream effects on deduction rules and transparency obligations
Escalation correctly recommends blocking CA and Germany rollout until legal review, while allowing Indonesia soft-launch — demonstrates nuanced phased rollout thinking
Identified the subtle trap: California earnings breakdown cannot be disabled (BR-3/AC-7) while Indonesia transparency is optional (BR-6) — surfacing the asymmetry
Confidence at 52 with well-articulated reasoning about legal uncertainty capping confidence — exactly the right posture for multi-jurisdiction legal complexity
Edge cases are strong — cross-jurisdiction operation, works council approval revocation, and mid-period fee changes all demonstrate deep domain thinking
Business impact correctly identifies that global rollout cannot be uniform and quantifies California violation penalties ($100+ per pay period per violation)

Could have more explicitly stated the trap that removing the feature in Indonesia is fine but removing it in California would be illegal — this is implied across BR-3 and BR-6 but not called out as a unified warning

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Jurisdiction-specific rules identified for all 3 jurisdictions — California mandatory transparency (BR-3), Germany works council gate (BR-4), Indonesia optional (BR-8). (2) Germany works council approval explicitly identified as a blocking pre-condition (BR-4 with invariant:true, escalation calling it a 'hard legal prerequisite'). (3) Driver classification data model difference identified (BR-7) with employee vs contractor distinction across jurisdictions. (4) Formal escalation for legal review present — 4 structured escalations including one explicitly stating 'Do not ship to any jurisdiction without jurisdiction-specific legal sign-off.' (5) No single global acceptance criteria set — AC-1 through AC-4 are jurisdiction-specific. (6) confidence.level = 45, well within the ≤60 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptional jurisdiction-specific analysis — each jurisdiction gets distinct business rules (BR-3, BR-4, BR-8) with correct invariant classifications (CA: true, DE: true, ID: false)
Works council requirement correctly identified as invariant with specific legal citation (Betriebsverfassungsgesetz §87) and treated as a hard blocker, not just a consideration
Driver classification (employee vs contractor) explicitly called out as a data model concern in BR-7 with invariant:true — misclassification identified as compounding legal violation
Acceptance criteria are jurisdiction-specific (AC-1 for CA, AC-2/AC-3 for DE, AC-4 for ID) — no single global rule set
Confidence level of 45 is appropriately low given multi-jurisdiction legal complexity
Edge case about works council approval revocation after deployment shows deep understanding — approval must be checked dynamically
Caught the subtle trap: tax withholdings differ by classification (BR-9) — showing withholdings not being made is materially false information
Business impact correctly identifies phased/jurisdiction-specific rollout necessity and quantifies California PAGA exposure
Cross-border driver edge case identified and correctly flagged for legal escalation
Four formal, structured escalations with options and recommendations — well beyond minimum escalation requirements
Edge case about negative net earnings in California correctly flags potential minimum wage violation — shows understanding of how transparency can surface compliance issues

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Jurisdiction-specific rules identified for all 3 jurisdictions — CA with AB5/employee classification and itemized wage statements (BR-3), DE with Works Council approval gate (BR-4), ID with contractor classification and voluntary transparency (BR-6). (2) Germany Works Council approval identified as a hard pre-condition blocking deployment (BR-4, AC-2, AC-8, escalation 3). (3) Driver classification data model difference (employee vs. contractor) explicitly identified and woven throughout business rules and acceptance criteria. (4) Formal escalation for legal review present (escalation 4) plus Works Council engagement escalation (escalation 3). (5) No single global acceptance criteria set — separate ACs for CA (AC-1, AC-6), DE (AC-2, AC-3, AC-8), and ID (AC-4). (6) Confidence level 52, well within the ≤60 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough jurisdiction-specific analysis — each jurisdiction gets distinct business rules, acceptance criteria, and risk assessment rather than a one-size-fits-all approach
Works Council (Betriebsrat) correctly identified as a legal gate with specific BetrVG §87 citation, not just a notification step — AC-2 and AC-8 enforce the blocking condition at the system level
Driver classification difference (employee in CA vs. contractor in ID) correctly identified as a data model concern that affects which payroll fields are visible and applicable
Subtle trap about mandatory-in-CA features surfaced via BR-2 (tip separation as wage theft if violated) and the reclassification edge case showing removal risks
Phased rollout recommendation explicitly stated in escalation 3: launch CA and ID first while DE Works Council engagement proceeds in parallel
Confidence at 52 with well-articulated high/low confidence areas and explicit assumptions — demonstrates appropriate humility given legal complexity
Edge cases are strong — cross-jurisdiction delivery, reclassification, conditional Works Council approval, and negative net pay scenarios all demonstrate deep domain thinking
Four formal escalations covering real-time definition, minimum wage calculation period, Works Council engagement, and legal review gating — all with options and recommendations
Business impact correctly identifies that global rollout cannot be uniform, with jurisdiction-specific risk levels (HIGH CA, HIGH DE, LOW ID)
